                       1 For coverage of series, see footnote 1, table B-2.
                       2 Beginning January 1965, data relate to railroads with operating revenues of $5,000,000 or more. May 1970: Average weekly earnings $170.82, average hourly earnings
                    $3.90, and average weekly hours 43.8; June 1970: Average weekly earnings $174.21, average hourly earnings $3.88, and average weekly hours 44.9. Corrections: Nov. 1969:
                    Average weekly earnings $164.49 and average weekly hours 43.4; Dec. 1969: Average weekly earnings $168.30, average hourly earnings $3.74, and average weekly hours 45.0.
                       3 Data relate to employees in such occupations in the telephone industry as switchboard operators; service assistant; operating room instructors; and pay-station attendants.
                    In 1968, such employees made up 32 percent of the total number of nonsupervisory employees in establishments reporting hours and earnings data.
                       4 Data relate to employees in such occupations in the telephone industry as central office craftsmen; installation and exchange repair craftsmen; line, cable, and conduit
                    craftsmen; and laborers. In 1968, such employees made up 32 percent of the total number of nonsupervisory employees in establishments reporting hours and earnings data.
                       5 Data relate to nonsupervisory employees except messengers.
                       6 Money payments only; tips, not included.
                       ' Data for nonoffice salesmen excluded from all series in this division.
                       * Not available,
                       p = preliminary.

                                                                 D-1:     Labor turnover rates in manufacturing
                                                                                      1959 to date

                                                                                     (Per 100 employees)
                                                                                                                                                   An nual
                           Year                     Feb.           Apr.        May        June            July   Aug.   Sept.   Oct.               average

                 Edition, U.S. Department of Labor, 1965.                                progressively to the current month by use of the sample trends.

                                                       Unemployment Insurance Data


                       Insured unemployment represents the number of persons           unpaid vacations. The rate of insured unemployment is the
                  reporting a week of unemployment under an unemployment in-           number of insured unemployed expressed as a percent of aver-
                  surance program. It includes some persons who are working part       age covered employment in a 12-month period ending 6 to 8
                  time who would be counted as employed in the payroll and             months prior to the week of reference. Initial claims are notices
                  household surveys. Excluded are persons who have exhausted           filed by those losing jobs covered by an unemployment insur-
                  their benefit rights and workers who have not earned rights to       ance program that they are starting a period of unemployment.
                  unemployment insurance. In general, excluded from coverage           A claimant who continues to be unemployed a full week is then
                  are those persons who worked in firms whose size excluded            counted in the insured unemployment figure.
                  them from the unemployment insurance laws, as well as many               Because of differences in State laws and procedures under
                  persons engaged in agriculture, domestic service, unpaid family      which unemployment insurance programs are operated, State un-
                  work, selected nonprofit organizations, State and local govern-      employment rates generally indicate, but do not precisely meas-
                  ment and self-employment. Also excluded from the insured un-         ure, differences among the individual States. Persons wishing to
                  employment count, but included as employed in the household          receive a detailed description of the nature, sources, inclusions

                  roll period because they were temporarily absent from their jobs     should address their inquiries to Manpower Administration,

                                                                Seasonal Adjustments

                      Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring sea-      approximation based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted
                  sonal movement which can be estimated on the basis of past           estimates have a broader margin of possible error than the
                  experience. By eliminating that part of the change which can         original data on which they are based, since they are subject
                  be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is possible to ob-       not only to sampling and other errors but, in addition, are
                  serve the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the            affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment proc-
                  series. However, in evaluating deviations from the seasonal          ess itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force
                  pattern—that is, changes in a seasonally adjusted series—            and establishment data are published regularly in Employment
                  it is important to note that seasonal adjustment is merely an        and Earnings.
